summary.brief: Taylor v. Brill. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Kimberly Taylor, the plaintiff in the lawsuit below, had a hysteroscopy performed by the defendant, Dr. Keith Brill.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Brill perforated Taylor's uterus and bowel during the procedure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Taylor reported escalating pain after the surgery and was twice transported to an emergency room via ambulance.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On the second trip, the attending doctor concluded her symptoms were consistent with an uncontrolled bowel perforation and performed an emergency surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Taylor alleged that Dr. Brill had breached the standard of care by piercing her uterine wall and small intestine during surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court erred in allowing such arguments and evidence at trial here.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We reverse the district court's judgment and remand this matter for further proceedings.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Informed consent evidence is inadmissible, and an assumption-of-the-risk defense is improper, in professional negligence suits when the plaintiff does not challenge consent.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Taylor's retained expert, Dr. Berke, clearly testified that the medical services Taylor received were reasonable and necessary and were caused by the perforations that arose from Dr. Brill's surgical procedure.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The district court abused its discretion in excluding this evidence, which affected Taylor's substantial rights.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient underwent a hysteroscopy performed by Dr. Brill. Post-procedure, she experienced escalating pain and was twice transported to an emergency room.

requestables.name: Abdominal imaging
requestables.description: Imaging to assess for bowel perforation

reveal.decision_impact: Critical for diagnosing the cause of escalating pain

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with escalating pain post-hysteroscopy

decision_points.clinical_context: During hysteroscopy, uterine and bowel perforation occurred
decision_points.prompt: What should have been done upon observing uterine perforation?

options.description: Stop the procedure and evaluate for bowel injury
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care requires immediate evaluation of potential complications

options.description: Continue the procedure without further evaluation
options.clinical_reasoning: Neglects the risk of further injury and complications

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant continued the procedure without evaluating bowel injury

expected_action_court.description: Stop the procedure and evaluate for bowel injury
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated this was the standard of care

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Continuing the procedure without evaluation increased risk of complications
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Immediate evaluation and management of perforation
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient required emergency surgery for bowel perforation

key_findings: Jury found in favor of Dr. Brill, denying all of Taylor's claims

factual_timeline.event: Patient transported to ER with escalating pain

treatments.treatment: Emergency surgery to correct bowel perforation

allegations.allegation: Failure to evaluate and diagnose bowel perforation

court_findings.finding: Jury found no breach of duty by Dr. Brill
